title: Introduction to Systems Programming
topics: Course goals: large software, modern methodology, C++, Python
Small programs vs. large systems and the consequences
What makes code correct and good (readable, no duplication, testable, maintainable, portable)
Why C++: based on C, object-oriented, industry standard, efficient, larger toolbox
Object-oriented programming as an intuitive concept enabling modularity and information hiding
History of C++ and its standards (C with Classes, C++98, C++11, C++14, C++17, C++20, C++23)
The Waterfall software development model and its shortcomings

key_concepts.definition: Small programs (10-1,000 lines) are written and used by one programmer and can be coded immediately; large systems (10,000+ lines) are built by teams for customers, require analysis/design, must be robust, and need long-term maintenance.
Code that is simple to read, follows unified conventions, avoids duplication, is easy to test, finds errors early, is intuitive, and ideally explains itself rather than relying on comments.
A programming paradigm that enables modularity and information hiding, making testing and maintenance easier.

A sequential development model (Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Maintenance) where each step must finish before the next; hard to use in practice because requirements and design cannot be fully fixed in advance.
Splitting development into iterations (sprints of 2-3 weeks), each implementing a small subset of features from task definition to testing, so the team can adapt to new requirements over time.

common_pitfalls: Assuming a large system can be fully designed before any code is written (waterfall fallacy)
Thinking comments are a substitute for clear, self-explanatory code
Believing that because 'anything can be done in C' there is no reason to learn C++

title: C++ Intro: Enhancements over C
topics: C vs C++ file extensions (.c vs .cpp)
Function pointers and passing functions as parameters
typedef and the using alias syntax
const pointers vs pointers to const
References as parameters, return values, and const references
Dynamic memory with new and delete
I/O streams (cin, cout, cerr) and file streams (ifstream, ofstream)

key_concepts.definition: A keyword that defines a new type and a set of named integer constants; names auto-convert to int and int can be cast to the enum. The names matter more than their actual values.

A scoped enumeration whose constants are accessed through the type name with :: (e.g., PowerState::Off) and which does NOT implicitly convert to/from int (requires an explicit cast). Solves name-clash problems between plain enums.
A keyword defining a new composite type made of named fields; a field can itself be a struct (nesting). Fields are accessed with '.' on objects and '->' on pointers.

A pointer that stores the address of a function's machine code plus its type (signature = number/type of arguments and return type); called with the () operator like a normal function, allowing functions to be passed as parameters and chosen at runtime.

typedef declares a new name (nickname) for an existing type; 'using Name = type' is the C++ alternative syntax. Used to shorten long type names, allow future type changes, improve readability, and hide implementation details.
A qualifier meaning a variable cannot be changed after initialization; unlike #define it is scoped and can be set at runtime, and unlike enum it can hold non-integer values.

Marking which objects change and which don't and keeping this consistent so the compiler (and reader) catch errors; constness propagates through all code touching a const object, and const cannot be removed without an explicit cast.

An alternative name (alias) for an existing variable; must be initialized, cannot be reseated to another target, and always refers to a legal object. Similar to a constant pointer but is not itself a manipulable object.
A reference through which the referenced object cannot be changed; unlike a non-const reference it can bind to a temporary value or constant, making it ideal for read-only parameters.
Defining several functions with the same name but sufficiently different parameters; the compiler picks the best fit for the given arguments.
Default values for parameters, specified only in the declaration, letting the function be called without those arguments.

C++ typed dynamic-allocation operators: new returns a typed pointer and initializes the object (unlike malloc which returns void* and leaves garbage); delete frees a single object and delete[] frees an array.

Idiomatic C++ input/output using cin (stdin), cout (stdout), cerr (stderr) with >> for input and << for output; no format specifiers are needed and new types can be supported. File I/O uses ifstream/ofstream, which close automatically at end of scope.

code_idioms: enum Season { SUMMER=1, FALL, WINTER=8, SPRING };
enum class PowerState { Off, On }; -> PowerState s = PowerState::Off;
double (*fptr)(double); fptr = cos; double y = fptr(90);
typedef int (*Callback)(int, char*); / using Point = Real[3];
int* const ptr (const pointer) vs const int* ptr (pointer to const) vs const int* const
int& r = i; // reference, must be initialized
void swap(int& a, int& b) { int t=a; a=b; b=t; } // reference parameters
int& max(int a[], int n) { ... } // reference return usable as LHS: max(a,5)=7;
void printNumbers(int n = 10); // default argument in declaration
int* p = new int(5); int* a = new int[10](); delete p; delete[] a;
cout << "x=" << x << endl; cin >> n;
ifstream f("code.txt"); if(!f){...} f >> code;
common_pitfalls: Expecting enum class values to auto-convert to int (they don't; need an explicit cast)
Confusing 'const pointer' (int* const) with 'pointer to const' (const int*)
Declaring a reference without initializing it (int& r; is illegal)
Trying to bind a non-const reference to a temporary or literal (swap(a, 3) fails)
Passing a const object to a function taking a non-const reference
Forgetting delete[] for array allocations (using delete instead of delete[])

title: Multi-File Projects, Preprocessor and the Compilation Process
topics: The compilation pipeline: preprocessor, compiler, linker
Declaration before use within a file
Splitting code across multiple source files
Declaring functions to call across files
Header files (.h) for interfaces and .cpp for implementation
Namespaces and the :: scope resolution operator

key_concepts.definition: Source code (*.cpp) plus headers pass through the preprocessor to produce processed code, which the compiler turns into object files (*.o); the linker combines object files and static libraries (lib*.a) into an executable.
To call a function/use a type across files it must be declared (or the type defined) before use; a declaration provides the signature so the body can appear later or in another file.
The interface (struct/function declarations) goes in a .h header included by users; the implementation goes in a .cpp source file that also includes the header.

A text-processing step that handles directives: #include pastes file contents, #define creates macros, and #if/#ifdef/#ifndef/#else/#endif do conditional compilation. gcc -E stops after preprocessing.
Angle brackets are used for standard/library headers; quotes are used for the project's own (user) headers.
A directive ensuring a header is included only once per translation unit, preventing duplicate declarations when files include each other.
A macro from <cassert> that, when debugging, evaluates a condition and terminates with an error message if it fails; when NDEBUG is defined (release), the condition is not evaluated at all.
A hierarchical scope (like folders) in which names can be declared; an item's full name is <namespace>::<item>, and a global-scope item is ::obj.

code_idioms: int array_max(int a[], int n); // forward declaration before use
#include "complex.h" // in complex.cpp to get the interface
#pragma once // at top of every header
#include <cassert> ... assert(arr != NULL && i >= 0 && i < size);
namespace fast_math { double sqrt(double); } -> fast_math::sqrt
gcc main.cpp utility.cpp testing.cpp // compile multiple files
gcc -E main.cpp // preprocess only
common_pitfalls: Calling a function defined later in the file without a prior declaration
Forgetting an include guard, causing 'multiple definition' errors from double inclusion
Assuming assert runs in release builds (it is disabled by NDEBUG)
Using <> for your own headers or "" for standard headers
Forgetting that #include literally pastes the whole file's contents

title: Classes, Encapsulation, ADTs and the Constructor/Destructor Lifecycle
topics: Motivation: a Complex number type = representation + operations
Implementing methods inside vs outside the struct (Class::method syntax)
Header/source split for a data type
static member variables and static member functions
Data structures as ADTs: implementing a Set of ints

key_concepts.definition: A data type is Representation + Operations. An Abstract Data Type (ADT) defines only the operations (the interface); the representation and implementation are hidden, and several implementations of the same ADT can exist.
A function declared inside a struct/class that operates on the object it is invoked through and has direct access to that object's members; it takes no space in the object itself.

Hiding a type's implementation so the private data is inaccessible and the type is used only through its public interface; changing only the private part does not affect user code (though recompilation may be needed).
Access specifiers: private members are accessible only by functions declared within that class; public members form the interface. Rule: never make a member variable public.

The class keyword is nearly identical to struct, differing only in default access: class members are private by default, struct members public by default. class is used for real data types, struct for simple aggregates.
An implicit constant pointer the compiler passes to every member function, pointing to the invoking object (c1.add(c2) becomes add(&c1, c2)); usually used implicitly but available explicitly.
A member function marked const at the end of its prototype; guarantees it does not modify its object and allows it to be called on const objects.

Member functions that read (getter) or update (setter) a field, giving controlled access (read-only, write-only, or both), enabling validation, and allowing a value to become a 'virtual field' computed on demand rather than stored.

A class variable not stored in any object: a single shared copy exists for the whole program even if no object is created; it is part of the class namespace, can be private, and must be defined in a source file (except static const initialized in-class).
A function that can access the class's private parts but is invoked without an object (Class::func()); it has no this pointer.

A special member function with the class's name and no return value, called automatically when an object is created; can be overloaded and take default arguments. Replaces error-prone init() functions.

A constructor taking no arguments, called when an object is created with no arguments; the compiler auto-generates one only if the class declares no constructors, and that generated one default-constructs member objects but leaves built-in members uninitialized.

A special member function ~ClassName() taking no arguments (only one per class), called automatically when an object is destroyed (goes out of scope, is deleted, or a temporary ends), used to free resources acquired by the constructor.
Syntax Ctor(args) : member1(v1), member2(v2) {} that initializes members before the constructor body runs; required for const members and members without a default constructor (e.g. references).
Initialization happens when a new object is created (Complex c2(c); or Complex c3 = c;); assignment modifies an existing object (c = c2;).

For type T, the constructor taking a single const T& that initializes a new object by copying an existing one. The compiler generates one (member-wise copy) even if other constructors exist, so every class has a copy constructor; a user-defined one is needed when the class manages resources like heap memory.

On construction, members are initialized in the order they are declared, then the constructor body runs; on destruction, the destructor body runs first, then members are destroyed in reverse order of declaration.

code_idioms: void Complex::add(Complex c) { re += c.re; im += c.im; } // Class::method definition
class Complex { double re, im; public: void init(...); }; // private-by-default
double Complex::abs() const { return sqrt(re*re+im*im); } // const method
Complex& Complex::add(Complex c) { ...; return *this; } // chaining via *this
static const int INITIAL_SIZE = 10; // in-class static const
int Complex::initCounter = 0; // static member definition in .cpp
Complex(double re, double im); // constructor declaration
Complex(double x, double y = 0); // constructor with default arg / conversion
~Array(); Array::~Array() { delete[] data; } // destructor
Array::Array(int n) : data(new int[n]), size(n) {} // initialization list
Array::Array(const Array& arr) : data(new int[arr.size]), size(arr.size) { for(...) data[i]=arr.data[i]; } // copy c'tor
Complex array1[10]; // default constructor called for each element
common_pitfalls: Making member variables public and thereby exposing/locking the implementation into the interface
Writing user code that assumes a specific representation (e.g., reading c.re) which breaks if the implementation changes
Relying on the compiler-generated copy constructor for a class that owns raw pointers (produces a shallow copy / double free)
Forgetting to delete dynamically allocated objects, causing memory leaks (*ptr2 is leaked)
Assuming the compiler still gives a default constructor after you define any other constructor (it does not)
Confusing initialization (Complex c3 = c;) with assignment (c = c2;)
Expecting members to be initialized in the initialization-list order rather than declaration order

title: Operator Overloading, Conversions and Iterators
topics: Motivation: make user types as convenient as built-in types
Which operators can/cannot be overloaded and the rules
Binary operators: member vs non-member form
Implementing + in terms of += to avoid duplication
User-defined conversions: converting constructors and conversion operators
Symmetric operators and the need for non-member functions
operator[] with const and non-const versions
Iterators: begin()/end(), a custom Iterator class, operator*, ++, ==, !=
Range-based for loops and functors preview

key_concepts.definition: Defining operators (like +, ==, <<) for user-defined types; C++ treats an operator @ as a function, so a@b means a.operator@(b) (member) or operator@(a,b) (non-member). At least one operand must be a user-defined type.

You cannot overload ::, ., .*, ?:, sizeof, typeid; cannot invent new operators; cannot change an operator's arity, precedence, or associativity. Overloaded operators should be used only when the meaning is natural and intuitive.

A binary operator can be a member taking one argument or a non-member taking two; =, [], (), and -> must be members. Non-member operators allow implicit conversion of both operands (needed for symmetry, e.g. 3 + r).

Because + creates a new object while += modifies in place, + is implemented using += to avoid duplication: operator+ copies *this then applies += and returns the result. operator+= returns a reference to *this.

A conversion where at least one type is user-defined, letting the compiler implicitly cast arguments. Defined either by a single-argument constructor (converting TO the class) or by a conversion operator like operator double() (converting FROM the class).

A keyword on a single-argument constructor or a conversion operator that prevents the compiler from applying it implicitly, avoiding ambiguous or surprising conversions. Rule of thumb: make single-argument constructors explicit unless a conversion truly makes sense.

A declaration inside a class that grants an external function (or class) access to the private section; it also declares the function so no separate forward declaration is needed (if the function takes at least one parameter of the class type).

Stream I/O operators overloaded as non-members because their first argument is the stream (ostream&/istream&), not the class object; they return the stream reference to allow chaining, and are often friends to access private data.

Compiler-generated by default (member-wise assignment); must be user-defined when the class manages a resource. A correct hand-written operator= releases old resources, allocates new ones, guards against self-assignment (if this == &s), and returns *this.
Implementing operator= by making a local copy of the argument then swapping its members with *this (via a friend swap); provides clean, duplication-free, exception-safe assignment.
The copy constructor, destructor, and assignment operator go together; if you need to write one you probably need all three. Intuition: Assignment = Destructor + Copy Constructor.

=default tells the compiler to generate a method it would not normally generate (or to document intent); =delete forbids a method (e.g., making a class non-copyable). Always mark special members =default if you want the compiler's default behavior.

The subscript operator, which must be a member; its parameter need not be int. Two versions are typically provided: a non-const one returning T& and a const one returning const T& for const objects; both should bounds-check and can throw out_of_range.

An object that lets you traverse a data structure like a pointer, hiding the internal representation. A minimal iterator supports operator*, operator++, and operator!= (and ==); begin() returns an iterator to the first element and end() to one-past-the-last.
operator++() is prefix (++it) and returns Iterator&; operator++(int) is postfix (it++), takes a dummy int parameter, saves a copy, increments *this, and returns the saved copy.

for (T x : container) works for any class providing begin()/end() that return a valid iterator (pointer or object with ++, != and *) and for all STL containers; declare the loop variable as a reference to avoid copying large objects.
An object of a class that overloads operator(), so it can be called like a function.

code_idioms: Complex operator+(const Complex& x, const Complex& y); // non-member binary
Complex operator-() const; // unary member operator
Rational& operator+=(const Rational& r); Rational operator+(const Rational& r) const; // + via +=
Rational::operator double() const { return double(numerator)/denominator; } // conversion operator
explicit Array(int size); explicit operator double() const;
friend bool operator==(const Rational&, const Rational&);
std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& os, const Rational& r) { ...; return os; }
Set& operator=(const Set& s) { if(this==&s) return *this; delete[] data; ...; return *this; }
Set& operator=(const Set& s) { Set temp(s); swap(*this, temp); return *this; } // copy-and-swap
WebServer(const WebServer&) = delete; void operator=(const WebServer&) = delete;
Person() = default;
int& operator[](int index); const int& operator[](int index) const;
Iterator& operator++(); // prefix   Iterator operator++(int); // postfix
for (int value : set) { sum += value; } // range-based for
for (const Student& s : students) { ... } // reference to avoid copy
common_pitfalls: Overloading operators in cryptic ways (e.g., set % 5 for 'contains') instead of named functions
Defining a binary operator as a member, which prevents converting the left operand (3 + r fails)
Allowing implicit conversions that create ambiguity or silent bugs (a = 5 building a temporary array)
Forgetting the self-assignment check in a resource-managing operator= (deleting data then copying from it)
Writing a copy constructor or destructor but forgetting operator= (violating the Rule of Three)
Returning by value where a reference is needed, or vice versa, for operator[]
Implementing operator+ without operator+=, duplicating logic
Forgetting that postfix operator++ takes a dummy int and must return a copy of the old value

title: Exceptions and Error Handling
topics: Strategies for reacting to errors and their drawbacks
Aborting, returning special values, status types, global error variables (errno)
The two contexts: detecting vs handling an error
The C++ exception mechanism: throw, try, catch
How an exception propagates up the call stack
Stack unwinding and automatic destructor calls
Cleaning up resources when a constructor throws

key_concepts.definition: Options include aborting the program (usually unacceptable), returning a special error value (not always possible, e.g. stoi), using a status return type (breaks composition and operator overloading), or a global error variable like errno (easy to ignore, may be overwritten). Each inflates code with checks that are easy to forget.

A function that detects a problem it cannot handle throws an exception; a function that can handle it uses try/catch; functions in between can safely ignore it. Separates the detecting context from the handling context.
Raises a new exception; the throwing function terminates immediately without returning a value, passing the exception to the caller.

A try block marks code that may throw; the catch block(s) right after it specify the exception type(s) handled and the recovery code. If no catch matches, the exception keeps propagating up the call stack; if it reaches out of main, the program terminates.

Several catch blocks can follow one try, each for a different type; catch(...) with an ellipsis catches any exception. The catch parameter is passed by value, so catch class/struct exceptions by reference to avoid a copy.

When an exception is thrown, the process of exiting functions until a matching catch is found; all local objects are destructed (their destructors called) exactly as when returning, so resources are freed automatically.
A class defined to represent a specific error type (e.g., class DivisionByZero {};), since exceptions are caught by type; usually simple but can carry data (e.g., OutOfRange storing the bad index).

Constructors and operators are ideal for throwing because they cannot return an error value. If a constructor throws, the object is not constructed and its destructor is NOT called, so anything already allocated must be freed explicitly before rethrowing (often via try/catch then throw;).

A destructor runs during stack unwinding while an exception may already be active, and there cannot be two simultaneous active exceptions, so throwing from a destructor is forbidden (leads to program termination).

code_idioms: throw string("cannot open file"); / throw -1; / throw DivisionByZero();
try { printGradesFile("grades.txt"); } catch (string s) { cerr << s << endl; }
catch (int n) { ... } catch (...) { cerr << "unknown error"; }
catch (const ElementExists& e) { /* by reference */ }
class DivisionByZero {}; // simple exception type
Map::Map() { cities = new City[N]; try { roads = new Road[M]; } catch(const bad_alloc&) { delete[] cities; throw; } }
common_pitfalls: Catching exceptions by value instead of by reference (extra copy; slicing for polymorphic exceptions)
Using exceptions as an ordinary return mechanism (e.g., Set::add throwing when an element already exists) which complicates and clutters user code
Forgetting to free resources allocated in a constructor before it throws (the destructor won't run)
Throwing from a destructor (illegal during stack unwinding)
Assuming a leaked/ignored return-code error is as safe as an exception (it is not; exceptions can't be silently ignored)
Ordering catch blocks so a base-class catch hides a more specific derived catch

title: Templates, Generic Programming, Functors and Lambdas
topics: Function templates and compile-time code generation
Template argument deduction rules and overload resolution
Two-phase template compilation and requirements on T
The typename keyword for dependent types
Only used template functions are compiled
Passing functors to a templated filter

key_concepts.definition: A built-in C++ mechanism where the compiler generates code from a pattern; the template parameter T is replaced by an actual type at compile time. Handled entirely at compile time and type-safe (adding a Student to a Set<int> is a compile error).

A function parameterized by a type: template<class T> T max(const T& a, const T& b). Can be called with explicit arguments (max<int>(a,b)) or with the compiler deducing them from the arguments (max(a,b)).

The compiler deduces template arguments only from function parameters; if it can't, they must be given explicitly. In overload resolution it prefers an exact-match ordinary function over a template, prefers a template over type conversions, and never applies conversions while deducing types.

template<class T> class Array { ... }; used by the compiler to create a distinct class per type; the argument is part of the class name (Array<int> vs Array<double> are unrelated). There is essentially no argument deduction for class templates in this course.

The compiler only does simple syntax checking on template code; real compilation happens when the template is instantiated with a specific type, at which point it checks that the type meets the template's requirements. Errors then produce long, hard-to-read messages.

For an instantiation to compile, T must satisfy whatever the used template code requires (e.g., copy-assignable, comparable with ==, etc.). Only template functions actually used are compiled, so T may not need to meet all requirements.
Needed before a dependent qualified type inside a template (e.g., typename Set<T>::Iterator) to tell the compiler the name refers to a type rather than a value.

Despite its use it takes no arguments; it has special behavior where the compiler re-applies -> to its return value, so it must return a type (pointer or object) on which -> can be applied again (it->x becomes it.operator->()->x).

An object of a class that overloads operator(), so it can be invoked like a function; unlike ordinary functions it can hold parameters as member variables (e.g., DividesBy(n), Between(min,max)) instead of needing a separate function per value.

An ad-hoc, nameless function written [capture](params) -> return-type { code }; the return type may be deduced if all returns give the same type. Its [] capture list captures local variables of the enclosing scope by value ([x]), by reference ([&x]), or automatically ([=] value, [&] reference, [this] for members).

The [] part of a lambda listing which enclosing local variables it captures and how; without capture a lambda has a simple pointer-to-function type, but capturing lets it use local variables. Class member variables are captured via [this] and accessed by reference through this.

code_idioms: template<class T> T max(const T& a, const T& b) { return a > b ? a : b; }
cout << max<int>(n1, n2); // explicit   cout << max(n1, n2); // deduced
template<class T> class Array { T* data; int size; public: T& operator[](int i); };
template<class T> T& Array<T>::operator[](int index) { ... } // member definition
template<class T> Set<T>::Set(const Set& set) : ... {} // templated constructor
typename Set<T>::Iterator Set<T>::begin() const { return Iterator(this,0); }
const T* operator->() const; // returns pointer for it->x
class DividesBy { int n; public: bool operator()(int x) const { return x % n == 0; } };
template<class Condition> Set filter(const Condition& c) const; // template method in template class
s.filter( [](int i) { return i % 2 == 0; } ); // lambda
[thresh](int i){ return i > thresh; } // capture by value; [&sum](int n){ sum += n; } capture by reference
common_pitfalls: Expecting the compiler to deduce a template argument that only appears in the return type (T* create() needs create<int>())
Assuming type conversions happen during template deduction (they never do; max(n1, d1) fails)
Treating Array<int> and Array<double> as related/assignable (they are distinct, unrelated types)
Forgetting the typename keyword before a dependent nested type in a template
Expecting a template to fully compile before instantiation (errors surface only at instantiation)
Using a type in Set<T> that lacks a required operation (e.g., no operator== or no operator<<)
Writing a separate function per parameter value instead of a parameterized functor
Forgetting to capture a needed local variable in a lambda (it won't be visible)

title: Inheritance, Polymorphism, Virtual Functions and UML
topics: Why one giant class or duplicated classes are bad
private vs protected access under inheritance
Subtyping: derived is-a base; B* usable as A*
Construction and destruction order with inheritance
Base class constructors are not inherited
Storing mixed types via base-class pointers
The dispatch problem and virtual functions
Pure virtual functions and abstract classes
Object slicing (no polymorphism with value variables)
dynamic_cast and querying the dynamic type
is-a vs has-a: inheritance vs composition (List/SortedList)
Exception class hierarchies and std::exception, logic_error, runtime_error

key_concepts.definition: A diagram describing the static structure of a system: classes with their attributes and methods and the relationships (e.g., associations) between them; fields/methods can be omitted for simplicity.

Deriving a new class from an existing one (class Engineer : public Employee) so the derived class has all fields and methods of the base plus its own; avoids duplication and the need to manually forward common functionality.

An access level between public and private: protected members are accessible within the class and any class derived from it, but not from outside. The derived class cannot access the base's private members even though it contains them.
A derived class is a subtype of its base, so it can be used wherever the base is acceptable; concretely B* can be used as A* and B& as A& (but not vice versa). Requires pointers or references.

Constructing: base members, base constructor body, derived members, derived constructor body. Destructing: reverse order. The base constructor can be called in the derived initialization list; the base destructor is called automatically.

Base class constructors are not automatically inherited; the derived class must define any constructors it needs and can forward to the base via the initialization list (Manager(name) : Employee(name) {}).

Providing a derived-class version of a base member function with the same signature; member variables cannot be overridden. A derived override can call the base version explicitly (Employee::giveRaise(...)).

A member function declared virtual in the base class; it uses dynamic binding, meaning the actual function called is determined at run-time by the object's real type, even when accessed through a base pointer/reference.
An annotation on a derived function indicating it is meant to override a base virtual; the compiler errors if no matching virtual exists in the base, catching signature mistakes.

The implementation of virtual dispatch: for each class with virtual functions the program stores an array of function pointers to the correct functions, and each object gets one pointer (at its start) to its class's vtable. A virtual call becomes an indirect call through the vtable.

A virtual function declared = 0 with no base implementation; a class with one or more pure virtual functions is abstract and cannot be instantiated (though pointers/references to it can exist). A derived class implementing all pure virtuals becomes concrete.
An abstract class that has only pure virtual functions; it describes the general behavior of a class without any concrete implementation (e.g., Shape with rotate/draw/area).

Destructors must be virtual when a class is used as a base with inheritance, so that deleting a derived object through a base pointer calls the correct destructor. Constructors are never virtual (you always construct a precise type).

Greek for 'having many forms': code that works on all types sharing a common base class without knowing each object's real type, performing a different action per actual runtime type without manual if/else. Enables adding new types without changing existing code.

Polymorphism only works through pointers or references; assigning a derived object to a base-type value variable (Employee emp = eng;) copies only the base part and loses the derived data and virtual behavior.

A virtual function (sometimes 'virtual copy constructor') declared in the base and overridden in each derived class to return a new copy of the true object type, letting you copy an object held by base pointer without knowing its concrete type or using if/else.

A compile-time-checked cast between types with a known conversion or an inheritance relation; when narrowing to a more specific type it is the programmer's responsibility to ensure correctness. It will not remove const and won't compile for unrelated types (unlike C-style casts).

A run-time-checked cast between pointers/references in an inheritance relation; returns the properly typed pointer if the object really is that derived type, or NULL if not. Requires a polymorphic base class (at least one virtual function).

Use inheritance only for a genuine is-a relationship where you need polymorphism (verify with the substitution test, e.g., SortedList fails a List insertFirst test so it is not a List). Otherwise prefer composition: hold an existing class as a private member (has-a / uses-a) to reuse its implementation without exposing a subtype relationship.

Exceptions can be caught through a base class, so grouping related exceptions under a common base lets one catch handle them all. Prefer deriving from the standard std::exception hierarchy (via std::logic_error for avoidable caller errors or std::runtime_error for run-time errors), which provides a virtual what() message.

code_idioms: class Engineer : public Employee { ... };
protected: int salary; // accessible to derived classes
Employee& ref = engineer; Employee* ptr = &salesPerson; // upcast OK
Manager::Manager(string name) : Employee(name), employees() {}
void SalesPerson::giveRaise(int a) { Employee::giveRaise(a + commissionRate*sales); } // override calling base
virtual void giveRaise(int amount); // virtual in base
void giveRaise(int amount) override; // in derived
virtual void giveRaise(int amount) = 0; // pure virtual -> abstract class
virtual ~Employee() {} // virtual destructor
static_cast<Manager*>(employees[i])->giveRaise(100);
const Engineer* eng = dynamic_cast<const Engineer*>(employees[i]); if (eng) {...}
virtual Shape* clone() const = 0; Shape* Circle::clone() const { return new Circle(*this); }
class SortedList { List elements; ... }; // composition instead of inheritance
class GradesException : public std::runtime_error { public: GradesException(const string& w) : std::runtime_error(w) {} };
common_pitfalls: Storing mixed derived objects by value in a container (must store base-class pointers)
Calling a base-pointer method expecting derived behavior without declaring the function virtual
Object slicing: Employee emp = eng; loses the derived part and virtual dispatch
Forgetting a virtual destructor in a polymorphic base, so deleting through a base pointer skips the derived destructor
Using C-style casts (unchecked, undefined behavior) instead of static_cast/dynamic_cast
Using dynamic_cast on a non-polymorphic base (no virtual functions) - it won't work
Trying to instantiate an abstract class (Employee e; is an error)
Using inheritance for a has-a relationship (SortedList inheriting List) that fails the substitution test
Relying on the compiler-generated copy constructor to copy polymorphic objects held by base pointer (need virtual clone())
Adding type fields and if/switch-on-type chains instead of using polymorphism

title: Software Design, UML Sequence Diagrams and Design Patterns
topics: Recap: why projects need design and iterations
Dividing projects into packages/modules with their own UML and namespaces
UML sequence diagrams for dynamic flows

key_concepts.definition: Dividing a large project into packages/modules, designing the interactions between them, giving each its own UML diagrams and usually its own namespace, and describing interactions with external systems/hardware.

A diagram describing one dynamic flow scenario in a system over time (unlike a class diagram which shows only static structure); main scenarios are described with several such diagrams, and they are useful for documenting bugs.

A general, reusable, named solution to a common software-design problem, dealing with relationships between classes/collaborators and abstractions on top of code; it is not a finished solution and is not concerned with specific algorithms or concrete implementations.

The influential 1994 book 'Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software' by Gamma, Helm, Johnson, and Vlissides, which catalogued design patterns and gave them a shared design vocabulary.

Behavioural (communication between objects: Iterator, Strategy, Command), Structural (class/object composition: Composite, Adapter, Decorator), and Creational (creating/accessing objects: Factory, Abstract Factory, Singleton).

Extracts an interchangeable behaviour into a separate class hierarchy (e.g., Behaviour with Aggressive/Cautious subclasses) and lets an object hold a pointer to a chosen strategy, so behaviour can change at runtime and new behaviours added without modifying the main class (avoiding a big switch on an enum).

Encapsulates each operation as an object in its own hierarchy (GameMove with apply() and undo()), so moves can be stored, logged, and undone uniformly, and new moves added without enlarging the main class.

Creates an ad-hoc wrapper class that makes one type conform to another's interface (PlayerAdapter : public GuiLib::Agent wrapping a Poker::Player), letting you reuse a third-party library without replacing your own well-suited types.

Wraps an object in another object of a common base type that adds behaviour and forwards to the wrapped object (StatisticsWindow/AnimatingWindow each holding a GuiWindow*), allowing features to be composed and toggled at runtime without subclass explosion.

Delegates object creation to a factory object so the client does not hard-code which concrete class to instantiate; an Abstract Factory has a virtual create() overridden by concrete factories (CryptoMessageFactory vs NoCryptoMessageFactory) to choose the product.

Ensures a class has only one instance with global access, by making the constructor private, exposing a static getInstance() returning a static local instance, and deleting the copy constructor and assignment operator. A singleton cannot (and should not) be subclassed and carries the drawbacks of a global object.

code_idioms: class Behaviour { virtual void makeMove(Game& game) = 0; }; // Strategy
void setBehaviour(Behaviour* b) { behaviour = b; } void makeMove(Game& g){ behaviour->makeMove(g); }
class GameMove { virtual void apply(Game&) const = 0; virtual void undo(Game&) const = 0; }; // Command
class PlayerAdapter : public GuiLib::Agent { Player* m_player; ... }; // Adapter
class AnimatingWindow : public GuiWindow { GuiWindow* window; void display(Game& g) override { window->display(g); animate(g); } }; // Decorator
class MessageFactory { virtual Message* create() const = 0; }; // Abstract Factory
static Game& getInstance() { static Game instance; return instance; } Game(const Game&) = delete; // Singleton
common_pitfalls: Relying on a class diagram (static) to capture dynamic behaviour - a sequence diagram is needed
Encoding behaviour with an enum + switch, which requires editing the class for each new behaviour (use Strategy)
Making a class ever-larger by adding every feature to it (use Command/Decorator to decompose)
Replacing your own well-fitted types wholesale with a library's types (use an Adapter)
Hard-coding which concrete product to construct with if/else (use a Factory)
Subclassing a singleton, or overusing singletons and inheriting all the problems of global objects

title: Type Inference with auto
topics: The auto keyword for variable declarations
Adding const and reference modifiers to auto
When to use auto and when not to
auto with iterators and clear-from-context types
auto in templates where the type isn't known
Naming and reference cautions with auto

key_concepts.definition: A keyword that declares a variable with the type of its initializer, letting the compiler deduce the type; especially useful for long STL type names like iterators. const and reference modifiers can be added (auto&, const auto&) to refine the deduced type.

Use auto when the exact type is unimportant (only what it can do matters, e.g. an iterator), when the type is clear from context and writing it adds clutter (auto C = inv(A)*B;), to avoid redundant type specification (auto b = new UIButton(...)), or when it is the only choice (deducing a template element type).

Avoid auto when you actually care about the variable's precise type (auto height = ...; then height/2 may be wrong) or when it harms readability; also give careful attention to variable names (auto status = ...) and remember to add reference modifiers (auto&) where needed to avoid unintended copies.

Functions can have their return type deduced (auto multiply(T x, U y) { return x*y; }); this requires all return statements to return the same type and that the entire function code be known when it is called, so it is mostly applicable to template functions.

code_idioms: auto iter = std::find(ids.begin(), ids.end(), 315085121);
auto& namesRef = names; // reference   const auto& idsRef = ids;
for (auto it = addressBook.begin(); it != addressBook.end(); ++it) { ... }
auto C = inv(A)*B; // type clear from context
auto button = new UIButton("OK", COLOR_GRAY);
template<class T, class U> auto multiply(T x, U y) { auto res = x*y; return res; }
auto& shapes = canvas.GetShapes(); // don't drop the reference
common_pitfalls: Using auto when the concrete type matters, leading to subtle bugs (integer vs double division)
Overusing auto so the reader can no longer tell what a variable is (readability harm)
Forgetting the reference modifier (auto instead of auto&), causing an unintended copy
Giving auto variables vague names that hide what they hold
Expecting return-type deduction with mixed return types or non-template code where it doesn't apply

title: Move Semantics and unique_ptr
topics: Motivation: avoiding unnecessary copies and enabling ownership transfer
References binding to lvalues; rvalue references (T&&)
Forcing a move with std::move / static_cast to rvalue reference
When std::move is and isn't needed
Move-based swap and safe copy assignment

key_concepts.definition: A mechanism introduced to solve two problems: avoiding unnecessary copies (e.g., copying a temporary result into a new object then destroying the temporary) and enabling ownership transfer (e.g., unique_ptr, file handles). 'Move is an optimization of copy.'

An lvalue is a value that can be assigned to (global/local variables, function parameters, std::cout); an rvalue cannot be assigned to (literals like 10 or "C++", and return values of functions/operators like height*width or Set::union(x,y)).

A reference that binds only to rvalues (int&& r = 3;), whereas a plain reference (T&) binds only to lvalues and a const reference binds to both. rvalue references let a function detect that its argument is a temporary that can be pilfered.

A constructor taking an rvalue reference Set(Set&&) that steals the source object's resources (copies its pointer/fields) instead of deep-copying, then leaves the source in a valid empty state (data = nullptr, size = 0), so no allocation or element copy occurs.

operator=(Set&&) that releases the current resources, takes over the source's resources, and nulls out the source; called when assigning from an rvalue (z = Set::union(x,y)), while copy assignment is used for lvalues (w = x).

A function that forces move semantics by casting its argument to an rvalue reference (equivalent to static_cast<T&&>), signalling the compiler to use the move constructor/assignment; it does not itself move anything. Needed when transferring ownership by assignment/initialization or by function argument, but not when returning a local value (the move is elided) or on the returned value.

The copy constructor, move constructor, destructor, copy assignment operator, and move assignment operator go together; if you need to write one, you probably need all five (the move-aware extension of the Rule of Three).

The compiler supplies a default move constructor/assignment (moving all fields) only if none of the five special functions is defined; they can be requested with =default. Otherwise the copy constructor is used, and built-in-type fields are always copied. (Note: naively wrapping every field in std::move in a hand-written move c'tor is 'no good' per the slides.)

A move-only smart pointer template owning a raw pointer: its copy constructor and copy assignment are =delete, it provides a move constructor and move assignment that transfer ownership and null the source, and its destructor deletes the owned object. Provides operator* and operator-> for pointer-like use.

code_idioms: int&& r = 3; // rvalue reference binds to rvalue
Set(const Set&); // copy   Set(Set&&); // move
Set<T>::Set(Set&& other) : size(other.size), maxSize(other.maxSize), data(other.data) { other.size = other.maxSize = 0; other.data = nullptr; }
Set& operator=(Set&&); // move assignment
col2.push_back(std::move(str)); // force a move
unique_ptr(const unique_ptr&) = delete; unique_ptr(unique_ptr&&); // move-only
unique_ptr<int> p3 = std::move(p1); // OK, calls move c'tor
process(std::move(my_ptr)); // pass ownership by argument
return eng; // move elided, no std::move needed on return
common_pitfalls: Trying to bind an rvalue reference to an lvalue (int&& r = i; fails) or a plain reference to a temporary
Forgetting to null out / reset the source object in a move constructor or move assignment (leaves it owning freed memory, causing double free)
Adding std::move when returning a local variable (unnecessary and can inhibit elision)
Copying a unique_ptr (deleted); you must std::move it
Forgetting std::move when moving a member in a move constructor (employee(std::move(other.employee))) since a named member is an lvalue
Assuming the compiler still generates default move operations after you define a destructor or copy operation (it won't)

title: Constructor Delegation and Inheriting Constructors
topics: Options: duplicate code, common init(), or constructor delegation
Inheriting base class constructors with using Base::Base

key_concepts.definition: One constructor calling another constructor of the same class in its initialization list before running its own body (Webcam(int w, double e) : Webcam(w, getHeight(w), e) {}); avoids code duplication versus a shared init() function. Once the delegated (first) constructor finishes, the object is considered fully constructed, so if the second constructor then throws, the destructor IS called.

Writing 'using Base::Base;' in a derived class tells the compiler to inherit all of the base class's constructors; each inherited constructor has the same parameters (including defaults) and does the same thing as in the base, and any new derived members are default-initialized.

Providing a default value directly on a non-static class member (bool m_isUserFacing = true;); the value is automatically assigned when the member is created, unless a constructor's initialization list specifies a different value.

code_idioms: Webcam(int w, double e = AUTO_EXPOSURE) : Webcam(w, getHeight(w), e) {} // delegation
using Webcam::Webcam; // inherit all base constructors
bool m_isUserFacing = true; FlashMode m_flashMode = FlashMode::Off; // default member initializers
common_pitfalls: Duplicating shared setup across multiple constructors instead of delegating
Forgetting that after delegation the object counts as constructed, so a later throw triggers the destructor
Assuming base class constructors are inherited automatically (they are not, without using Base::Base)
Expecting a default member initializer to override an explicit value given in the constructor's initialization list (the initialization list wins)

title: Testing Code and Creating Unit Tests
topics: Black box vs white box testing
White box test code inside the code (assertions, NDEBUG-guarded prints)
Self-checking tests that report pass/fail and the reason
What to test: boundary cases, duplicates, random comparison
Levels of testing: unit, integration, system
Unit testing a class (one test per interface function)
Writing code that is easy to test
Regression testing and continuous integration idea

key_concepts.term: Black box vs white box testing
key_concepts.definition: Black box tests exercise a function only through its interface without seeing its internals; white box tests may inspect or embed code within the implementation (e.g., assertions and NDEBUG-guarded diagnostic prints inside the function).

Static testing examines code without running it (code reviews - time-consuming but very effective - and static code-analysis tools that flag suspicious code like uninitialized objects or unreachable code). Dynamic testing runs the code, via preprocessor-controlled checks (assertions) or test drivers that run the program and check its behavior.

Manual testing runs the program and looks for bugs (simple but slow, error-prone, and repetitive); automatic testing writes code that verifies correctness (more code up front but runnable many times at the touch of a button). Prefer investing in automatic tests; some manual testing remains.
A test that automatically determines whether it passed or failed and, on failure, supplies the reason (e.g., comparing the result vector to the expected one and printing the diff).
Re-running tests after a change to make sure nothing that previously worked has broken; automated tests make this effective and cheap.

System tests check the overall system in a usage scenario (hard to automate); integration tests check interactions between modules (important when different teams build them); module/unit tests check a single unit (typically a class) and are simple, with failures easy to localize.

Test one module (a class), divided into several tests - a good rule of thumb is one test per function in the interface. Each test calls the module with some input (including error cases, not just normal behavior) and checks that the returned values are correct.

The real challenge is writing code that is easy to test: modules with few/no dependencies, members isolated with private/protected, no modifiable global objects, short single-task functions, minimized side-effects, and reduced duplication via helper functions, inheritance, or composition.

code_idioms: bool equalVectors(const vector<int>& a, const vector<int>& b) { if (a.size()!=b.size()) return false; for (...) if (a[i]!=b[i]) return false; return true; }
void testEqualVectors(const vector<int>& a, const vector<int>& b, const string& name) { if (equalVectors(a,b)) cout<<name<<": PASSED"; else { cout<<name<<": FAILED"; printVectorDiff(a,b); } }
#ifndef NDEBUG ... diagnostic prints ... #endif // white box in-code test
void testSortEmpty() { vector<int> vec(0), sorted(0); sort(vec); testEqualVectors(vec, sorted, "testSortEmpty"); } // boundary case
int main() { testSort(); testSortWithDuplicates(); testSortEmpty(); testSortSingleton(); return 0; }
verifyEqual(colors.getSize(), 3, "testSetGetSize");
common_pitfalls: Testing with only one input and assuming the function is correct
Ignoring boundary cases (size 0 or 1) and duplicate values
Only testing normal behavior and neglecting error handling
Relying solely on manual testing, which is slow, repetitive, and error-prone
Writing code with many dependencies, global mutable state, or long multi-task functions that is hard to unit test
Forgetting that assertions/NDEBUG-guarded checks are disabled in release builds
